meta.title: SG-SCD-1: Workbooks, Datasets, Embedding and Performance

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a Sigma workbook?

options.text: A collaborative analytical document containing pages and data elements
A standalone connection configuration for warehouse credentials
A published dataset schema that enforces column-level governance
A scheduled export job that sends reports to email recipients

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a Sigma workbook is the core collaborative surface for analysis and presentation — it contains pages, charts, tables, and controls that users interact with.

**B** is incorrect because connection configurations are managed in the Connections section of Administration, not in workbooks.

**C** is incorrect because published datasets serve as the governed semantic layer; a workbook is the analytical document built on top of such datasets.

**D** is incorrect because scheduled exports are a workbook feature, not the primary purpose of the workbook itself.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: A team wants one date selector to filter multiple workbook elements at once. What should they configure?

options.text: A materialization schedule tied to the date column
A control target linking the date control to each element
A user attribute that maps the date to a warehouse role
A JWT claim embedding the date range in the signed URL

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because controls in Sigma can be configured with targets that specify which workbook elements they filter, allowing one date selector to drive multiple charts and tables simultaneously.

**A** is incorrect because materialization schedules persist cached results on a time basis and are unrelated to interactive filtering.

**C** is incorrect because user attributes are used for row-level security and personalization, not for wiring UI controls to elements.

**D** is incorrect because JWT claims are used for authorizing embedded access, not for cross-element filtering within a workbook.

[Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

questions.stem: Why would a team publish a Sigma dataset?

options.text: To bypass the warehouse and store data inside Sigma's own storage layer
To convert every workbook element into a REST API endpoint
To restrict access to only Admin-role users when opening the workbook
To create a reusable governed semantic layer that many workbooks can build on

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because published datasets define shared business logic — joins, column definitions, metrics — that multiple workbooks can reference, improving consistency and governance across the organization.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma always queries the underlying cloud data warehouse; it does not maintain its own data storage layer.

**B** is incorrect because datasets are analytical models, not REST endpoints. The Sigma API provides programmatic access but is separate from datasets.

**C** is incorrect because dataset publishing is about reuse and governance, not about restricting workbook access to admins.

[Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

questions.stem: What is the main advantage of Sigma's visual join builder?

options.text: It physically denormalizes and materializes all joined tables in the warehouse automatically
It generates a published dataset from the join and enforces column-level encryption
It replaces workbook permission controls with row-level security based on join conditions
It lets users define join types and key conditions without hand-writing SQL

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because the visual join builder presents join configuration — join type, key columns, and join conditions — as a graphical interface, making it accessible to users who are not comfortable writing SQL while still generating efficient warehouse-native queries.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma does not force physical denormalization or automatic materialization; the join is computed at query time unless the developer explicitly schedules materialization.

**B** is incorrect because the join builder does not encrypt columns; column-level security is managed through permission settings and user attributes.

**C** is incorrect because permissions are managed separately from join logic; join conditions define data relationships, not access control.

[Overview of joining data](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/joins-overview)

questions.stem: Which Sigma feature supports manual business-user writeback to the connected warehouse?

options.text: Input tables
Workbook controls with a list-value source
Version-tagged workbook templates
Scheduled materialization jobs

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because input tables are designed specifically for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases, allowing business users to type values that are persisted back to the warehouse.

**B** is incorrect because workbook controls — such as dropdowns or date pickers — filter or parameterize the analytical view; they do not write new records to the warehouse.

**C** is incorrect because version tags track content milestones for rollback and audit, not for data writeback.

**D** is incorrect because materialization schedules cache existing query results as warehouse tables; they do not accept user-entered data.

[Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

questions.stem: When is materialization most useful in Sigma?

options.text: When you want to share a workbook with users who do not have warehouse credentials
When repeated workbook queries are expensive and a cached warehouse table will improve response time
When you need to enable input table writeback for business users
When a control needs to filter multiple elements simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because materialization persists expensive intermediate query results as warehouse tables on a schedule, reducing the compute cost and improving response time for workbooks that repeatedly run the same heavy query.

**A** is incorrect because access to workbook data is governed by Sigma permissions and warehouse roles, not by materialization; users without warehouse credentials cannot bypass that through materialization.

**C** is incorrect because input table writeback is a distinct feature that allows data entry; it does not depend on materialization being enabled.

**D** is incorrect because cross-element filtering is accomplished using control targets, not materialization schedules.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes Sigma query execution?

options.text: Sigma exports data to a local browser cache and runs all computations client-side in JavaScript
Sigma requires users to write and submit raw SQL for every workbook element
Sigma compiles workbook operations into warehouse-native SQL and pushes execution to the connected warehouse
Sigma runs a proprietary in-memory engine inside the Sigma cloud and never touches the warehouse after initial load

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Sigma's core design principle is query pushdown — it translates workbook interactions into SQL that executes directly on the connected warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, etc.), leveraging the warehouse's compute and scale.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma does not copy full datasets to the browser; only result sets are returned to the UI after warehouse execution.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma's value is that users can build analyses without writing SQL; the platform generates SQL automatically.

**D** is incorrect because Sigma does not maintain a proprietary in-memory engine that replaces the warehouse; the warehouse is the execution engine.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: A workbook is slow because a massive join happens before any filters are applied. What is the best first optimization?

options.text: Enable materialization with a daily schedule so the joined data is pre-cached
Split the join into multiple workbook pages to distribute the load
Convert the join to a published dataset to reduce per-workbook join overhead
Apply the filter before the join to reduce row volume entering the join operation

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because reducing the number of rows before the join significantly lowers the amount of data the warehouse must process. Applying selective filters early is the most impactful first step because it directly shrinks join input size and enables better pushdown.

**A** is incorrect because materialization caches the result of the existing expensive query; it does not restructure the query to avoid the expensive join. It can help for repeated reads but does not fix the underlying inefficiency.

**B** is incorrect because splitting the join across workbook pages does not change the SQL generated for any individual element; each page would still join the full dataset.

**C** is incorrect because publishing to a dataset moves the join definition but does not add a filter; the same expensive full-scan join would still execute at query time.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Which Sigma function category is most appropriate for expressions such as `DateAdd` and `DateDiff`?

options.text: Date and time functions
Lookup and relationship functions
Aggregate functions
Conditional functions

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` operate on date and time values — adding intervals or computing the difference between two dates — which places them squarely in the date and time function category.

**B** is incorrect because lookup functions retrieve matching values from another element or dataset based on a key; they do not manipulate date values.

**C** is incorrect because aggregate functions like `Sum`, `Count`, or `Avg` reduce many rows to a single value; date arithmetic operates at the row level.

**D** is incorrect because conditional functions like `If` and `Coalesce` evaluate logical expressions; `DateAdd` and `DateDiff` are not conditional in nature.

[Function index](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/functions-index)

questions.stem: What problem does the `Lookup` function primarily solve in Sigma?

options.text: Running a window calculation such as cumulative sum or rolling average across a result set
Retrieving a related value from another element or result set based on a matching key
Counting distinct values across multiple columns in the same table
Replacing null values with a fallback from a separate data source

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the `Lookup` function performs a key-based value retrieval from another element — similar to a VLOOKUP in spreadsheets — allowing a column in one element to pull in a matching value from a different data source or result set.

**A** is incorrect because running totals and rolling averages are handled by window functions such as `RunningSum` or `MovingAvg`, not by `Lookup`.

**C** is incorrect because counting distinct values uses aggregate functions like `CountDistinct`; `Lookup` does not aggregate.

**D** is incorrect because null replacement is handled by conditional functions like `Coalesce` or `IsNull`; while `Lookup` could return a fallback as a side effect, that is not its primary purpose.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Which element type is most appropriate for showing one headline KPI such as month-to-date revenue?

options.text: A pivot table element with a single measure and no row groups
A bar chart with a single series and no dimensions
A KPI card element
A data table filtered to one row

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because KPI cards are purpose-built to display a single headline metric prominently, with optional comparison values such as period-over-period change, making them ideal for executive-level summaries.

**A** is incorrect because a pivot table with one measure and no row groups would display the metric but is designed for multi-dimensional cross-tabulation, not headline display.

**B** is incorrect because a bar chart with one series and no dimensions would render a single bar — technically showing the value, but charts are designed for comparisons and trends, not single-metric prominence.

**D** is incorrect because filtering a full data table to one row carries all the overhead of a table element and does not give the visual prominence a KPI display requires.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Why might a developer link workbooks together?

options.text: To reuse context parameters and enable navigation across related analytical experiences
To merge the two workbooks into one shared dataset automatically
To synchronize version history across both workbooks
To grant the target workbook access to the source workbook's warehouse connection

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because workbook links allow a developer to pass filter values or parameters from one workbook to another and create drill-through navigation paths, giving users a connected analytical experience across separate content.

**B** is incorrect because linking workbooks does not merge them or create shared datasets; the two workbooks remain independent and manage their own data sources.

**C** is incorrect because version history is tracked independently per workbook; linking does not synchronize versions between them.

**D** is incorrect because warehouse connections are managed in Administration and are not shared or granted through workbook links.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: A user should be able to explore and build ad hoc analyses but not administer the entire organization. Which Sigma role is the closest fit?

options.text: Viewer, because Viewers can explore workbooks and create new ones in their personal space
Creator or Explorer, because these roles support analysis and content authoring without organization-wide administration
Admin, because ad hoc analysis requires elevated data access permissions
There is no built-in role for this; a custom role must be created via the API

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Creator and Explorer account types allow users to build analyses, create workbooks, and explore data without having the organization-wide administrative capabilities of the Admin role.

**A** is incorrect because the Viewer role is focused on consuming published content; Viewers typically cannot create or edit workbooks, which makes it too restrictive for ad hoc analysis.

**C** is incorrect because Admin is the most privileged role, with access to organization settings, connections, and user management — far broader than what an analyst needs.

**D** is incorrect because Sigma provides built-in account types that cover this use case; a custom API-created role is not required.

[Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of user attributes in Sigma security design?

options.text: To define the warehouse connection credentials for each individual user
To configure the appearance and branding of the Sigma interface per team
To support dynamic row-level filtering and tenant personalization based on user identity
To set rate limits on API requests made by each user account

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because user attributes are key-value pairs assigned to users or teams that can be referenced in dataset filters and embed parameters, enabling dynamic row-level security so each user only sees the data relevant to their identity or tenant.

**A** is incorrect because warehouse credentials are configured in Connections by Admins and applied at the connection level, not per user via user attributes.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma's branding and theming is controlled in Organization settings, not through user attributes.

**D** is incorrect because API rate limits are not configured through user attributes; they are governed by Sigma's platform policies and the REST API authentication layer.

[User attributes](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/user-attributes)

questions.stem: What does Sigma version history help teams do?

options.text: Schedule materialization runs on a fixed cadence
Sync workbook changes to an external Git repository automatically
Generate signed embed tokens for each workbook version
Review prior workbook states and restore to an earlier version if needed

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because version history captures a timeline of workbook edits, allowing teams to review what changed over time and restore the workbook to a previous state if a change introduced errors or regressions.

**A** is incorrect because materialization schedules are configured separately in the workbook's materialization settings; version history does not control when caching jobs run.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma does not natively sync workbook changes to external Git repositories; version history is Sigma's own internal change tracking.

**C** is incorrect because embed tokens are generated at runtime by the embedding application server; they are not tied to workbook version history entries.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Which approach is used for client-side embedded Sigma analytics?

options.text: Generate a signed embed URL or JWT on the application server and load it in an iframe
Export the workbook to a static HTML file and host it on the customer's CDN
Materialize every workbook page to a Cloud Storage bucket before embedding
Copy the workbook data into the embedding application's own database first

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Sigma embedding is based on generating a signed URL (or JWT-based token) server-side with appropriate claims such as user email, team, and user attributes, then rendering it in an iframe within the host application.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma workbooks are dynamic, live-querying documents; they cannot be meaningfully exported to static HTML while retaining interactive filtering and real-time data.

**C** is incorrect because materialization is an optional performance optimization for caching expensive queries; it is not required before embedding and does not produce an embeddable artifact.

**D** is incorrect because Sigma always queries the connected warehouse at query time; data is not copied into the embedding application's database.

[Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

questions.stem: Why should embed tokens include an expiry?

options.text: Sigma requires an expiry to determine when to refresh the workbook's materialized cache
Token expiry limits the window in which a stolen or intercepted embed URL can be misused
Without an expiry the embedded workbook cannot load user attributes from the identity provider
Sigma's API rate limiter uses token expiry to determine the allowed request quota

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a short-lived expiry (typically seconds to minutes) ensures that even if a signed embed URL is intercepted or leaked, the attacker has only a narrow window before the token becomes invalid and access is denied.

**A** is incorrect because materialization scheduling is independent of embed token expiry; the two features are unrelated.

**C** is incorrect because user attributes are embedded in the token's claims at generation time; they do not require a live connection to an identity provider at load time, and expiry does not control this flow.

**D** is incorrect because API rate limits are governed by Sigma's platform policies based on API keys and organization settings, not by embed token expiry.

[Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

questions.stem: Which scenario is a good fit for an input table?

options.text: A sales manager manually enters quarterly targets that should be stored back in the warehouse alongside actuals
A developer wants to pass a tenant identifier through a signed embed URL
An analyst wants to cache an expensive aggregation query for reuse
An admin wants to configure a new warehouse connection for the organization

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because input tables are designed for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases — scenarios where business users need to add records that are persisted back to the warehouse, such as budgets, targets, or annotations.

**B** is incorrect because passing tenant context through a signed embed URL uses user attributes and JWT embed parameters, not input tables.

**C** is incorrect because caching an expensive aggregation query is achieved through materialization, not input tables.

**D** is incorrect because warehouse connections are configured in the Connections section of Sigma Administration, not through input tables.

[Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

questions.stem: A workbook uses complex calculations across the same billions-row dataset every morning. Which feature is most likely to reduce repeated runtime cost?

options.text: Add a second workbook page that duplicates the calculation for easier review
Convert all numeric measures to text columns to simplify type handling
Schedule a materialization that pre-computes the expensive result and stores it as a warehouse table
Publish the workbook to a shared workspace so users run queries simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because materialization pre-computes and persists the expensive query result as a native warehouse table on a schedule (e.g., overnight). When users open the workbook in the morning, Sigma reads from the cached table instead of re-running the billions-row computation, dramatically reducing cost and latency.

**A** is incorrect because duplicating the calculation on a second page doubles the query cost rather than reducing it.

**B** is incorrect because converting measures to text changes the data type but does not reduce the rows scanned or the complexity of the underlying computation.

**D** is incorrect because having multiple users run the workbook simultaneously increases the number of warehouse query executions; it does not reduce per-query cost.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: What is the difference between workspace permissions and workbook permissions?

options.text: Workspace permissions apply only to scheduled exports while workbook permissions apply to interactive viewing
They are always identical because workbook permissions inherit from the workspace and cannot be overridden
Workbook permissions grant broader organization-wide access while workspace permissions are scoped to a single asset
Workspace permissions grant access across all content in the workspace, while workbook permissions target specific workbooks with potentially different access levels

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because workspace-level permissions apply broadly to all content inside that workspace, while individual workbook permissions allow more granular access control — for example, granting one team Viewer access at the workspace level but Editor access on a specific workbook.

**A** is incorrect because workspace permissions control access to all content types within the workspace, not just scheduled exports.

**B** is incorrect because permissions can be set independently at the workbook level and can differ from the workspace-level grant; they are not always identical.

**C** is incorrect because this reverses the relationship — workspace permissions are broader (covering many assets), while workbook permissions are scoped to a single asset.

[Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

questions.stem: Which calculation type is best for running totals or rankings within Sigma?

options.text: Window functions such as running average or rank
Aggregate functions such as Sum and Count applied to the full table
Lookup functions that retrieve values from another element
Conditional functions such as If and Switch applied row by row

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because running totals and rankings are analytical patterns that require computing a value for each row relative to surrounding rows within a defined order or partition — which is exactly what window functions like `RunningSum`, `Rank`, and `CumulativeDistribution` provide.

**B** is incorrect because standard aggregates like `Sum` collapse all rows into a single total; they do not produce per-row running values.

**C** is incorrect because lookup functions retrieve a matching value from another element based on a key; they are not designed for ordered running calculations.

**D** is incorrect because conditional functions evaluate logical expressions and return one value or another; they do not accumulate values across rows in order.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Why should a developer prefer a published dataset over rebuilding the same joins in many workbooks?

options.text: Published datasets remove the need for a warehouse connection and can run offline
A published dataset centralizes business logic, improving governance and ensuring consistent metric definitions across all workbooks
Published datasets automatically materialize their joins on a daily schedule
Published datasets grant every user who accesses them full Admin permissions on the source tables

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because defining joins, column aliases, and metrics once in a published dataset means every workbook that builds on it shares the same business logic. If the definition changes — such as a revenue metric formula update — it propagates to all dependent workbooks automatically.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma always queries the connected warehouse; published datasets do not bypass the warehouse or enable offline execution.

**C** is incorrect because materialization is a separate optional feature that must be explicitly configured; publishing a dataset does not automatically schedule materialization.

**D** is incorrect because publishing a dataset does not grant users elevated warehouse permissions; access is still governed by Sigma workspace and workbook permissions.

[Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

questions.stem: An embedded workbook must apply a tenant-specific filter automatically for each signed-in customer. Which design best supports this?

options.text: Pass user or tenant context through signed embed parameters and apply user attributes to drive row-level filtering in the dataset
Create one separate workbook per customer and generate a unique embed URL for each workbook
Use a workbook control with a hardcoded list of tenant values that users select manually
Materialize a separate warehouse table per tenant and point each embed at the appropriate table

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because embedding context plus user attributes supports dynamic tenant-aware filtering at scale. The application server encodes the tenant identifier in the signed embed URL; Sigma maps it to a user attribute that drives a dataset filter, so each customer sees only their own data without requiring separate workbooks or materials.

**B** is incorrect because maintaining one workbook per customer is operationally unscalable — any change to the workbook layout must be replicated across every customer copy.

**C** is incorrect because a hardcoded control list requires the customer to select their tenant, which is a security risk (they could select another tenant) and poor UX.

**D** is incorrect because creating and managing a separate materialized table per tenant is highly operationally complex and does not scale as the customer base grows.

[Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

questions.stem: Which role is usually most restricted in Sigma?

options.text: Creator
Explorer
Viewer
Admin

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the Viewer account type provides the most restricted access — Viewers can consume published workbooks and interact with controls but cannot create, edit, or save workbooks, making it the least privileged of the common user-facing account types.

**A** is incorrect because Creators have broader capabilities than Viewers, including the ability to build and publish workbooks.

**B** is incorrect because Explorers can also explore and interact with data more deeply than Viewers, such as editing in their personal Explore space.

**D** is incorrect because Admin is the most privileged account type, with full control over organization settings, connections, and user management.

[Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

questions.stem: Why is query pushdown important in Sigma?

options.text: It removes the need for users to write SQL and generates Python scripts instead
It copies the entire source table into Sigma's own cache before any filtering occurs
It limits the number of concurrent queries a user can run to prevent warehouse overload
It sends computations to the warehouse engine so data is processed close to where it lives, using the warehouse's scale and speed

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because pushdown executes SQL directly on the connected warehouse — Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, or Redshift — leveraging that platform's distributed compute, indexes, and caching rather than transferring raw data to Sigma servers for processing.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma generates SQL for warehouse execution, not Python scripts; the goal is warehouse-native computation, not replacing SQL with another language.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma does not copy entire tables into its own layer before filtering; predicates are pushed into the warehouse query, reducing data movement.

**C** is incorrect because query pushdown refers to where computation happens (warehouse vs. Sigma), not to concurrency throttling.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: What is the main tradeoff of materialization?

options.text: Perfect real-time freshness with zero maintenance overhead
Faster repeated reads at the cost of managing refresh schedules and accepting some data staleness between runs
Elimination of all warehouse costs because the data no longer queries the source
Automatic propagation of updated permissions from the source table to the materialized result

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because materialized results improve query speed and reduce compute costs for repeated reads, but they introduce a staleness window — the cached table reflects the data as of the last scheduled refresh, not the current warehouse state.

**A** is incorrect because materialization is by definition not real-time; data is only as fresh as the last completed refresh run, so freshness and zero maintenance cannot both be guaranteed.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse costs are not eliminated — the materialization job itself consumes warehouse compute when it runs, and the cached table occupies warehouse storage.

**D** is incorrect because materialized tables are separate physical objects; permission changes on the source table are not automatically reflected in the materialized result without explicit reconfiguration.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: A developer wants a toggle that switches a chart between Gross Margin and Revenue. Which Sigma feature is the best fit?

options.text: A published dataset with two separate metric columns pre-computed
A workbook control — such as a segmented button — whose value drives a conditional calculation in the chart
A materialization schedule that alternates between two pre-cached versions of the chart
An input table where the user types the desired metric name each time

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because controls such as a list, radio button, or segmented button can be configured to drive chart logic through a conditional calculation — for example, `If([Control] = "Gross Margin", [Gross Margin], [Revenue])` — allowing the user to toggle the displayed metric interactively.

**A** is incorrect because having two columns in a published dataset does not provide the interactive toggle; the user would see both columns simultaneously rather than switching between them.

**C** is incorrect because materialization schedules cache static snapshots; they cannot alternate between different metric views based on user interaction.

**D** is incorrect because requiring users to type a metric name in an input table is error-prone and a poor UX pattern compared to a dedicated interactive control.

[Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

questions.stem: What can the Sigma REST API help automate?

options.text: Programmatic management of workbooks, members, connections, and scheduled exports
Running SQL queries directly on the connected warehouse from outside Sigma
Configuring warehouse cluster sizes and autoscaling policies
Generating Sigma embed tokens without a signing secret

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Sigma REST API exposes endpoints for managing workbooks, members, teams, connections, datasets, and scheduled exports — enabling CI/CD pipelines, provisioning scripts, and integration workflows to automate Sigma operations.

**B** is incorrect because the Sigma API manages Sigma content and operations; SQL queries are executed through the warehouse connection, not through the Sigma REST API.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse cluster configuration is managed in the warehouse platform itself (e.g., Snowflake or BigQuery), not through the Sigma API.

**D** is incorrect because embed tokens always require a valid embed client ID and secret for signing; the API does not bypass this security requirement.

[Sigma API documentation](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/api-reference)

questions.stem: A company needs separate visibility by customer account inside the same workbook. Which approach is most scalable?

options.text: Create a separate workbook for each customer account and share it directly with their Sigma users
Use a workbook control with a dropdown listing all customer accounts, relying on users to select their own
Use row-level filtering driven by user attributes so each user automatically sees only their account's data
Export the data to CSV per account and distribute via email on a schedule

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because user-attribute-driven row-level security filters data at query time based on the authenticated user's identity, so a single workbook securely serves all customer accounts — each user sees only their account's rows without any manual selection.

**A** is incorrect because maintaining a separate workbook per customer account is operationally unscalable; any workbook update must be repeated across every copy.

**B** is incorrect because relying on users to select their own account from a dropdown is a security risk — a user could select a different account and see another customer's data.

**D** is incorrect because CSV email distribution is a manual, batch-oriented process that does not provide the interactive, real-time analytical experience the question implies, and does not scale to many accounts.

[User attributes](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/user-attributes)

questions.stem: Which warehouse platforms can Sigma commonly connect to?

options.text: Only Snowflake, because Sigma was originally built exclusively for Snowflake
Only self-hosted databases accessible via a private VPN gateway
Only cloud platforms that run on AWS; GCP and Azure are not supported
Cloud data warehouses such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, and Databricks

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because Sigma supports connections to multiple cloud data warehouses and lakehouses including Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Databricks SQL, giving organizations flexibility in their warehouse choice.

**A** is incorrect because while Sigma's origins are closely associated with Snowflake, it now supports multiple warehouse platforms and is not limited to Snowflake.

**B** is incorrect because Sigma connects to cloud data warehouses via standard drivers and APIs; it is not limited to databases behind private VPN gateways.

**C** is incorrect because Sigma supports warehouses running on GCP (BigQuery) and Azure (Synapse, Databricks) in addition to AWS-hosted warehouses.

[Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

questions.stem: A developer wants to troubleshoot why a workbook calculation is expensive. What is the most useful first step?

options.text: Inspect the generated SQL and query plan to identify filters, joins, or functions that prevent efficient pushdown
Publish the workbook as a template and let Sigma auto-optimize the SQL
Add more user attributes to restrict which rows each user can query
Convert the workbook element into an input table and re-enter the values manually

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because performance troubleshooting starts by understanding the shape of the SQL Sigma generates. Examining the query reveals which operations — such as a cross join, a missing filter push, or a non-sargable function — are causing the warehouse to do unnecessary work.

**B** is incorrect because publishing as a template does not trigger any SQL optimization; the same query logic is preserved in the published version.

**C** is incorrect because adding user attributes adds row-level filter predicates, which can reduce scanned data as a side effect, but it is not a diagnostic step and should not be the first move when troubleshooting a specific expensive calculation.

**D** is incorrect because converting a calculation to an input table means replacing a computed result with manually entered data, which defeats the purpose of the calculation and is not a performance fix.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: Why might a team tag workbook versions before a release?

options.text: Version tags automatically materialize the workbook's data at the point of tagging
Version tags mark known-good milestones that can be referenced for rollback, audit, or embedding specific published states
Version tags enable embed tokens to be generated without a signing secret
Version tags copy the workbook to a separate workspace for QA testing

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because version tags create named checkpoints in a workbook's history. Teams can tag a version before deploying to ensure they can roll back to a known-good state, and embedded analytics can reference a tagged version to pin the embed to a stable workbook release.

**A** is incorrect because version tags capture a snapshot of the workbook definition, not the underlying data; they do not trigger materialization or persist query results.

**C** is incorrect because embed tokens always require a valid signing secret; version tags have no bearing on that security requirement.

**D** is incorrect because version tags do not copy workbooks to another workspace; they are metadata applied to the workbook's history within its current location.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of an embed sandbox or test environment?

options.text: To validate the embedded experience — including token generation, user attribute filtering, and layout — before rolling out to production users
To permanently replace the production embed with a read-only view that prevents data changes
To automatically generate a signed embed token without requiring a server-side application
To convert the embedded workbook into a standalone HTML export for offline distribution

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a sandbox or test environment lets developers verify that embed token signing, user attribute injection, dynamic filtering, and iframe rendering all work correctly before exposing the integration to real end users in production.

**B** is incorrect because a test environment is a temporary validation context, not a permanent production replacement; it does not alter data permissions.

**C** is incorrect because signed embed tokens must always be generated server-side using the embed client secret; a sandbox does not bypass this requirement.

**D** is incorrect because Sigma embeds are live, interactive experiences that query the warehouse in real time; they cannot be converted to static HTML exports.

[Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

questions.stem: Which action best reduces workbook latency when users regularly open a page with a highly selective control?

options.text: Materialize the entire unfiltered dataset so the page loads from a cache regardless of the control value
Add more visual elements to the page to distribute the rendering workload
Apply the control-driven filter as early as possible in the query path to reduce the rows the warehouse must process
Switch the control from a dropdown to a free-text box to avoid loading the list of values

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because applying a highly selective filter early in the query allows the warehouse to prune rows before any expensive joins or aggregations, minimizing the data volume processed and returning results faster.

**A** is incorrect because materializing the full unfiltered dataset does not help when the control is highly selective — the cache still needs to be scanned in full unless the materialization is also partitioned or filtered by the control value.

**B** is incorrect because adding more visual elements increases the number of queries Sigma must execute, which adds latency rather than reducing it.

**D** is incorrect because switching to free-text avoids the list-of-values query but may worsen overall usability and query performance if users enter imprecise values.

[Intro to control elements](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/intro-to-control-elements)

questions.stem: What should be rotated periodically for connections that use service-account credentials?

options.text: The workbook version tags associated with the connection
The user attributes attached to the service account in Sigma
The materialization schedules linked to datasets using that connection
The credentials or secrets for the service account to reduce the exposure window of any compromised key

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because rotating service-account credentials (passwords, API keys, or OAuth secrets) on a regular schedule limits the damage if credentials are compromised — an attacker who obtains an old credential cannot use it after rotation.

**A** is incorrect because version tags are metadata on workbook history and have no connection to credential security.

**B** is incorrect because user attributes drive row-level filtering and personalization; rotating them would change the data each user sees, not improve credential security.

**C** is incorrect because materialization schedules control when cached results are refreshed; they are separate from the authentication credentials used by the connection.

[Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

questions.stem: Which function family is most appropriate for replacing nulls with fallback values?

options.text: Window functions such as running sum or lag
Lookup functions that retrieve values from another element
Conditional functions such as `Coalesce` or `IsNull`
Date functions such as `DateAdd` or `DateTrunc`

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because `Coalesce` returns the first non-null value from a list of expressions, and `IsNull` checks whether a value is null — both are specifically designed for null-handling logic, such as replacing null with a default value, a zero, or a string like 'Unknown'.

**A** is incorrect because window functions like running sum or lag operate over ordered sets of rows to compute positional or cumulative values; they are not designed for null replacement.

**B** is incorrect because lookup functions retrieve matching values from another element based on a key; they do not test for null or substitute fallback values.

**D** is incorrect because date functions manipulate date and time expressions; they are not used for null replacement in general-purpose columns.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about embedding Sigma are accurate? (Select TWO)

options.text: Signed JWTs or embed secrets can authorize embedded access without requiring the end user to have a Sigma account
Embed parameters can pass dynamic context such as tenant filters and user attributes into the embedded workbook
Every workbook must be fully materialized before it can be embedded
Embedding a workbook removes the need to configure any permission or security settings for the embedded content

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Sigma embedding uses signed credentials (embed client ID and secret) to authorize iframe access, allowing organizations to embed analytics for users who are not Sigma-licensed accounts.

**B** is correct because the embed URL can include signed parameters that map to user attributes, control values, or filter overrides, enabling dynamic personalization and tenant isolation per viewer.

**C** is incorrect because materialization is an optional performance optimization; it is not a prerequisite for embedding. Workbooks query the warehouse live when embedded.

**D** is incorrect because embedded workbooks still inherit the security configuration set in Sigma — user attributes, workspace permissions, and dataset-level filters all apply during embedded sessions.

[Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO practices most improve governed reuse in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: Publish shared datasets that centralize join logic and metric definitions
Rebuild all joins independently in every workbook so each team controls its own definitions
Centralize common calculations and column aliases in reusable dataset assets
Grant every user full Admin rights so they can access any dataset they need

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because shared published datasets ensure all workbooks reference the same join logic, table definitions, and governance rules, reducing divergence across teams.

**C** is correct because defining common calculations, renamed columns, and standardized metrics in a dataset means those definitions are available to all workbooks that use it, avoiding duplication and inconsistency.

**B** is incorrect because rebuilding joins independently in each workbook increases the risk of drift — teams may implement the same join with subtle differences — and creates a maintenance burden when source schemas change.

**D** is incorrect because granting full Admin rights to all users violates the principle of least privilege and introduces security and governance risks unrelated to dataset reuse.

[Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

questions.stem: Which TWO controls are most relevant when securing multi-tenant analytics in Sigma? (Select TWO)

options.text: User attributes mapped to tenant identifiers for dynamic row-level filtering
Granular workspace or workbook permissions that control which teams can access each asset
Workbook color themes applied per tenant for visual differentiation
Scheduled materialization runs that pre-cache data per tenant each night

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because user attributes enable dynamic row-level security — each embedded or logged-in user automatically sees only the rows matching their tenant, enforced at query time by Sigma.

**B** is correct because workspace and workbook permissions control which users and teams can view, edit, or manage each content asset, preventing one tenant's users from accessing another tenant's workbooks.

**C** is incorrect because color themes are cosmetic customizations; they do not enforce data isolation between tenants.

**D** is incorrect because materialization schedules optimize query performance; they do not provide any security boundary between tenants, as all tenants would read from the same cached table unless additional filtering is applied.

[User attributes](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/user-attributes)

questions.stem: Which TWO steps are most likely to improve a slow Sigma workbook? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply selective filters before large joins to reduce the rows processed by the warehouse
Schedule materialization for expensive repeated query results
Disable all workbook controls to prevent users from triggering additional queries
Duplicate the workbook into multiple folders so queries run in parallel on separate copies

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because pushing selective filters early in the query path reduces the number of rows the warehouse joins and aggregates, which is the most impactful type of SQL optimization for large datasets.

**B** is correct because materializing expensive intermediate results as warehouse tables means repeated workbook loads read pre-computed data rather than re-executing costly calculations each time.

**C** is incorrect because disabling controls removes useful interactivity without addressing the root cause of poor performance — the underlying query structure or data volume.

**D** is incorrect because duplicating a workbook into multiple folders results in the same SQL being executed independently on each copy; it does not reduce individual query cost and adds maintenance overhead.

[Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO capabilities make input tables useful for planning workflows? (Select TWO)

options.text: Business users can manually enter values such as budgets or targets directly in the workbook
Entered values are written back to the connected warehouse for persistence and downstream use
Input tables automatically generate signed embed tokens for the data they contain
Input tables delete version history entries as new values are entered

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because input tables surface a spreadsheet-like data entry experience inside the workbook, allowing business users to enter planning values — budgets, targets, adjustments, annotations — without leaving the Sigma environment.

**B** is correct because the entered values are written back to a warehouse table, making them available as a persistent data source that can be joined against actuals or referenced by other workbooks in downstream analysis.

**C** is incorrect because signed embed tokens are generated by the embedding application server using the Sigma embed secret; input tables are not involved in token generation.

**D** is incorrect because input tables do not delete version history; Sigma retains a history of workbook changes independently of input table operations.

[Input tables overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/input-tables)

questions.stem: Which TWO workbook design choices best support a polished analyst experience? (Select TWO)

options.text: Configure controls to target relevant elements so filters apply meaningfully and intuitively
Organize related content across clearly named pages with consistent navigation
Permanently hide all controls even when users need to filter the data
Avoid published datasets so each page builds independently from raw warehouse tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because controls configured with appropriate targets make workbooks intuitive — users understand which filters affect which charts, reducing confusion and supporting self-service analysis.

**B** is correct because organizing content across well-named pages with logical navigation allows analysts to find what they need quickly and understand the workbook's analytical flow.

**C** is incorrect because permanently hiding controls that users need to filter data frustrates analysts and defeats the self-service purpose of interactive workbooks.

**D** is incorrect because avoiding published datasets means each page must independently rebuild joins and metric logic, increasing maintenance burden, inconsistency risk, and query complexity.

[Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

questions.stem: Why is granular sharing generally safer than granting broad workspace access to everyone?

options.text: Broad workspace access always triggers additional warehouse compute, increasing cost
Granular sharing limits access to only the content each user actually needs, following the principle of least privilege
Granular sharing automatically replaces row-level filtering for all workbooks in the workspace
Broad workspace access disables version history for all workbooks in the workspace

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because least-privilege access control — giving users access only to the specific assets they need — reduces the blast radius if an account is compromised and prevents accidental exposure of sensitive analytical content.

**A** is incorrect because access permissions govern who can view content, not the warehouse compute cost of running queries; broader permissions do not inherently increase cost.

**C** is incorrect because sharing permissions control who can open a workbook; they do not automatically configure row-level filtering within it. Row-level security requires user attributes and dataset filters.

**D** is incorrect because version history is a workbook feature that persists regardless of the sharing model applied to the workspace.

[Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

questions.stem: Which Sigma capability is most useful when an application must export workbook results programmatically on a schedule?

options.text: Workbook version tags that snapshot the workbook definition at export time
User attributes that restrict which rows are included in the exported data
The Sigma REST API, which supports programmatic scheduled exports of workbook content
Materialization schedules that pre-cache the data and make it available for download

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the Sigma REST API exposes export endpoints that allow an external application or scheduler to trigger workbook exports programmatically, in formats such as CSV, XLSX, or PDF, on a custom schedule.

**A** is incorrect because version tags mark workbook definition milestones; they do not trigger or automate data exports.

**B** is incorrect because user attributes control data visibility at query time; they are a security mechanism, not an export automation tool.

**D** is incorrect because materialization pre-caches query results as warehouse tables; the cached data is not automatically pushed or exported to an external destination.

[Sigma API documentation](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/api-reference)

questions.stem: Why should a Sigma developer avoid unnecessary cross-joins in workbook modeling?

options.text: Cross-joins are not supported in Sigma's visual join builder and must be written in raw SQL
Cross-joins prevent materialization from being scheduled on the affected workbook
Cross-joins disable workbook controls from filtering the joined elements
Cross-joins multiply row counts between tables, producing extremely large intermediate result sets and expensive warehouse queries

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because a cross-join produces the Cartesian product of two tables — a table with M rows joined to one with N rows produces M × N output rows. For large tables this creates massive intermediate result sets that consume significant warehouse compute, memory, and cost, often unintentionally.

**A** is incorrect because Sigma's visual join builder supports multiple join types; while cross-joins may require specific configuration, they are not entirely unsupported in Sigma.

**B** is incorrect because the presence of a cross-join does not prevent materialization from being configured; materialization can persist any query result, including expensive ones.

**C** is incorrect because workbook controls and filters operate on the output of the query regardless of join type; cross-joins do not disable control filtering.

[Overview of joining data](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/joins-overview)
